perturbation.original_text: 1.17 "NDA" means a New Drug Application filed with the FDA for the Combination Product, requesting permission to place a drug on the market in accordance with 21 C.F.R. Part 314 and all supplements filed pursuant to the requirements of the FDA, including all documents, data and other 3 information concerning the Combination Product which are necessary for FDA approval to market a product in the United States.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.17 "NDA" means a New Drug Application filed with the FDA for the Combination Product, requesting permission to place a drug on the market at the FDA's discretion. Supplements are optional and based on internal company policies, including only information the company deems necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition aligns with the FDA's requirements for NDA submissions. The modified definition contradicts the FDA's mandatory requirements for specific data and supplements, making approval discretionary and undermining regulatory compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 314 - Applications for FDA Approval to Market a New Drug
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR Part 314 specifies the mandatory information, data, and supplements required for NDA approval. The modified text incorrectly suggests that these are optional, which is a direct contradiction of FDA regulations.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts FDA requirements as outlined in 21 CFR Part 314, by suggesting that supplements and data submission are optional rather than mandatory. The original text correctly references the need to comply with FDA regulations. The change makes compliance discretionary, going against the mandatory nature of FDA submissions. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of 21 CFR Part 314, which the LLM explanation uses as a basis for contradiction. The modification undermines regulatory compliance, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 LEO shall indemnify and hold GALEN and its agents, directors, officers and employees and representatives harmless from and against any and all Losses which they may at any time incur by reason of any Action or Proceeding brought by any Governmental or Regulatory Authority or other third party against GALEN arising out of or resulting from (a) any misrepresentation, breach of warranty or non-fulfilment of or failure to perform any agreement or covenant made by LEO in this Agreement, (b) the use of the Combination Product in any clinical trial, or (c) any other negligent act or omission of LEO.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 LEO shall indemnify and hold GALEN harmless from any Losses which they may at any time incur resulting from (a) any major misrepresentation, breach of material warranty, or significant non-fulfilment of any major agreement made by LEO in this Agreement, (b) grossly negligent usage of the Combination Product in any clinical trial, or (c) intentional acts of omission of LEO.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides broad indemnification. Modifying it to 'major misrepresentation,' 'material warranty,' 'grossly negligent usage,' and 'intentional acts' limits the scope of indemnification significantly, requiring a higher threshold for liability and introducing ambiguity. This could contradict legal standards regarding negligence and breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-322.1 addresses agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence. By adding qualifiers such as 'major,' 'material,' and 'intentional,' the modified text attempts to limit liability in a way that could be construed as an attempt to circumvent responsibility for negligence, potentially violating the spirit, if not the letter, of the law.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-322.1 Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-322.1. Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces qualifiers like 'major misrepresentation,' 'material warranty,' 'grossly negligent usage,' and 'intentional acts,' which narrow the scope of indemnification compared to the original broad terms. This shift attempts to limit LEO's liability for negligence. NY GOL § 5-322.1 states that agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence are void and unenforceable under certain circumstances. By adding qualifiers, the modified text appears to circumvent responsibility for certain types of negligence, potentially conflicting with the law's intent to prevent parties from avoiding liability for their negligence as stated in the provided snippet. The original text held LEO liable for 'any' misrepresentation, breach, or negligent act, whereas the changed text raises the bar, arguably attempting to exempt LEO from liability for lesser forms of negligence.

perturbation.original_text: XIII - GOVERNING LAW THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNED BY AND CONSTRUED IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE INTERNAL LAWS OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK APPLICABLE TO AGREEMENTS MADE AND TO BE PERFORMED ENTIRELY WITHIN SUCH STATE, WITHOUT REGARD TO THE CONFLICTS OF LAW PRINCIPLES OF SUCH STATE OTHER THAN SECTIONS 5-1401 OF THE NEW YORK GENERAL OBLIGATIONS LAW.
perturbation.changed_text: XIII - GOVERNING LAW THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNED BY AND CONSTRUED IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE INTERNAL LAWS OF THE STATE OF NEW YORK, WITH REGARD TO THE CONFLICTS OF LAW PRINCIPLES OF SUCH STATE, INCLUDING SECTIONS 5-1401 OF THE NEW YORK GENERAL OBLIGATIONS LAW.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause explicitly excludes conflicts of law principles except for 5-1401. The modified clause includes conflicts of law, which can change how the contract is interpreted if there are connections to other jurisdictions. This alteration could undermine the original intent to apply purely New York internal law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct contradiction of § 5-1401 itself (which allows parties to choose NY law), including all conflict of law principles fundamentally changes the application of New York law to the contract, as the original text intended to bypass them.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by including conflicts of law principles, which the original text explicitly excluded. This inclusion undermines the original intent to apply solely New York internal law, potentially altering the contract's interpretation and enforceability. The Law Explanation supports that including all conflict of law principles fundamentally changes the application of New York law to the contract, as the original text intended to bypass them. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 5-1401, allows parties to choose NY law to govern their rights, which is what the original clause does. The modified clause introduces uncertainty and deviates from this selection, potentially contradicting the parties' initial choice.
